speaker: interviewer
text: This is Bernard Presser, tape number two. Bernard, you were telling me about your friends from school. Did you remember the name of the other friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were also telling me about the German occupation on September 1st, 1939. Can you continue with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I told you-- you before, I was in the trench with a-- a leader of patrol, from the second-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --area, company, or whatever was it. And by this time, the day before, they announce it by radio that they would be around. Just only to be prepared, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was-- we were sitting with him in the trenches. And all of sudden, they find out when the plane was coming. He was completely white. What's happened? [? I said to him. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me, they are not our planes. They are German planes. And there was-- they put-- they-- they tried to-- bombard the-- the station. But fortunately, it was [? sunny ?] and [? this ?] gone to the streets over the station was-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many planes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was very many of them. I couldn't tell you. Anyway, but I was running, put my head down. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the army from September, '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- later, I didn't stay with-- in Cracow for the one-- to the one last train, Panzer train. It was required with them I have to-- I have to listen to the commander of the station. We had been traveling to Tarnow. And after-- because of-- the-- the-- the bridge was damaged, we have to leave. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I was-- was with my friend, the other one, who was with me in the-- in-- in the same duty. We had been wandering, trying to avoid that [INAUDIBLE] if we can, the German. We have only once, we catch up with them. [? What about ?] [? this-- ?] how this-- very far in the east, there were a very big village. They have quite a big population of Jewish, Russian, and Poland. Russian-- not Russians-- Ukrainian and Poland-- Polish. And when we come over, that-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] There was a kind of-- of an [? issue ?] in the village, there was a kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Of sort of government, or something of this kind. And he was-- he was actually the-- the chief of that one. And he tell us, you have to change. Open-- put away the-- your army-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --dressing. And so just-- so I-- I-- what do we have to do? What you getting at? So he told-- he told us, Jewish will go to the Jewish people, Polish to Pole-- Polish people, Ukrainian to Ukrainian to [? training. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- to here, that he asked me to-- to go to a place, the [? oldest ?] [? mansion. ?] First of all, they didn't want to open the door. The Polish chap who was with me, he was already in civil clothing. He told me, I will shoot them, but we have only five-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Five bullets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. And you couldn't have shot it with-- to the [INAUDIBLE]. So-- but he discharged one-- one of them. When he discharged, they opened the door. When I tell him, listen, I-- I have a new German-- Polish dress. I will give you everything. Give me something. He-- He put me in such a scene that you couldn't believe. I come back. We had been walking back, in a-- on a side road. We had plenty of tobacco, because-- [? Begging, ?] Cracow, with the-- after the first-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --day, there was one carriage with-- with tobacco, destroyed. So we kept it, whatever we have, to the-- to the-- our [NON-ENGLISH]. It was in the [NON-ENGLISH]. There was a problem in the military that the [NON-ENGLISH] is the bread. Bread container. The bread container is--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you say it in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The bread container is supposed to-- to-- to-- to be sold by [NON-ENGLISH]. In Polish, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And we had plenty of that one, that stuff. Very-- very-- very-- valuable. In-- especially in the-- in a easily, in a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you return to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 of September, when I come to my mother, shop, my mother didn't recognize me. What I can do for you? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find in Cracow? How had things changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a-- a change. Because there was you can see that-- that you are restricted. All of a sudden, you couldn't-- the eating restriction, the Jewish people have to-- straight away, to have the armband. They are calling you to-- to-- to different work. In the autumn it was not so bad, but in the winter, it was very big winter, it was very bad winter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- I couldn't remember-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --remember why. But all of a sudden, all the people have the money to spend. You know? I don't know how this happened. So with just what I'm tell you, my mother was selling 25 vials of beer in one week. And we had to have, 100 liters for winter, on this time. And so on. I was-- I tried to-- to have connection.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the same chap who was with me all the time, he was going to the [? monopoly ?] to-- to-- to buy the liquor, the-- the-- the-- the spirit. Because, he was-- he was telling that he would took over the business. And he-- he was giving very big supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had-- I just met him in a-- because-- but this is 50 years-- 57 years after. I couldn't remember this very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother's business--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --affected by the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for one-and-a-half year, I believe. Later she get-- she get a-- a German [? trustee. ?] There was Polish people who-- who take the list of German,

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Volksdeutsche ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Poles took over the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What type of work were you assigned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- when I come from the-- the war, I was penniless. And I was behind the wages, in [INAUDIBLE]. So I come over there to-- for the money. Well, the-- the chap who-- who was a manager told me, well, you have some goods. Sell it. And we will have the money. So I went to-- to a few [? opposites, ?] who wasn't-- who are not Jewish, business. And they-- they take the advantage. Because they couldn't get this, what we have, on the stock. And I brought, kind of good money to-- to the business. And we divided it. And later, we was-- were working for [INAUDIBLE] factory. There were other factory, sold soft drinks or soda water at the price. Fortunately, one of the working, who was working for his father, like-- with father, he took me-- he tried to teach me all those things. And because I was a little bit, maybe, more [? electrical ?] minded, I can catch up with all the things, what I have to do, repair and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work were you doing at the soda factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --mixing this-- the-- preparing the syrup. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Caring that we have all the ingredients, all we need. It was-- It was very difficult to get carbon, CO2-- CO2. And I-- By chance, I-- I met a Polish engineer who told me he can make it in a very smart way. But he can always make, about 40 kilos a day, or 60 kilo day, when he will improve. And I was one of the very few who hasn't got restriction on this [INAUDIBLE]. Because of the connection what I have before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How long did you think the war would last at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: For us, there was no-- no way to-- to know what-- what we hear from the friends, Polish friends who has been listened to the communicate from ABC, from London, that everything is-- is good. Everything is coming. They will come. No, no, no. Nothing is coming. So you have very bad feeling that this-- but we know the English people have a pact with Poland-- Poland, that when the Germans will attack the Poland, they were in the war. They were in the war, but they're staying in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK. What was the community's response-- the Jewish community's response, in Cracow, to the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were frightened. They were frightened. Because you know, the-- the Orthodox people have to making some-- to-- to hide in their beard. When they find out they have it, they put it on the-- cut it on the street. It was very popular. Besides that one that I told you, they were taking us to different work. And there was one very interesting point, of this one. They call him Quesa Volta, because quesa is cheese, and Volta was the name of the company, what he is riding the truck. Then, rather. And he's-- was running and picking the young people-- he'd send everybody to the truck. It was 5, 10, whatever he can catch. Because where he was climbing [? over ?] [? the hill, ?] we all [? go ?] everybody's thinking, OK, the Volta is coming. So everybody running away. Whoever he catch up, he want money. You paid the money, he let you go. And when you haven't got the money, he take you out-- outside the-- the-- on the freeway, about 10, 15 kilometers, and threw away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just leave you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he catch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was-- he was not-- that was only money. He was not a danger for life or something of this kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he catch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I haven't been catched by him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a good runner? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I was not living, by this time, in [? Kazmir. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any contact with the German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some interactions?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in 1942, when the-- the first time the German army was engage in winter, and there was plenty of casualties. And they had been-- they haven't got the [? wars. ?] They haven't got that. So they had plenty of-- I cannot [INAUDIBLE]-- flight or something. [INAUDIBLE] Anyhow, they have to go through certain routine. They are coming to one place, take all the-- the dress and everything. Also the-- out-- out for the washing [? covers ?] and so on. They go out, out, and they get a new one. And one of this place where they taken after that they have been cleaned was a school next to our place. And I had quite funny experience with them. First of all, they want to-- they want beer. Well, I tell him, when you want beer, you have to give me the-- the beer. I will bottle for you. So the-- the captain of this-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --regiment or whatever, that's in a school, he told me, just tell me what you need. And next day-- the next day, I got it. It was trouble. Because we haven't got the corks, and so on, but I make that [? now. ?] We make quite a good profit on that. 100 liter of beer, when I give him 50-- and 50%. I was selling on black market. I have a very interesting one point. The people, the German-- German people-- [INAUDIBLE], doesn't want back to the-- to the front. And they tried to-- to-- and they were saying, I have a case. One of them come to our factory. They was going there. And they, you know, they spilled the water. We couldn't-- you couldn't [? clean up. ?] That's the part of the business. The water was on the floor. The floor was concrete. And he told-- he ordered someone to got his shoes. And he walk on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder, what are you doing? Oh, I have to catch a cold. Why? I don't-- I don't want to-- to go back to-- to the front. I-- I want to-- to help [INAUDIBLE]. They all say they will help. These people have been quite friendly, all of them. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The young people doesn't-- doesn't want to do it. Well, it was very heavy winter. And the snow was-- heaps of snow was on the-- on the footpath.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which winter? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before that, I want to go back to 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, let's go to 1941, when the ghetto was erected in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: '41. Did you have any notice that there would be a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that they were building a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they-- as I remember, there was a gossip, first. Later, through the Jewish-- Jewish-- what do they call it, [NON-ENGLISH]. The people who had been in charge of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrat, right. That's correct. Mm, they-- from them, we know that the ghetto would be erected. And the date was fixed. And now everybody knows about what all. So March, 1941, we have to-- there was a time when he have to go, to go there. If we don't go, we are arrested, and so on. We have-- this comfort, that we have a horse and a cart. We will take as much what they can. First of all, I was sent in the ghetto to find the place. Because otherwise, they put three, four families-- families in-- in one dwelling. I find a small room, maybe 4 by 6, or maybe less. And it's, you know, with-- without water running. Without-- only there was a oven. And toilet was outside and so on. I-- But I find a room. And I ask-- I-- through connection, friends, they give me the-- the room. It was very comfortable-- in this condition. It was very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: For one half year, we are living by ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Frania and me. [LAUGHS] And her brother come back from Russian, from Lviv. When the German took over Lviv, he-- he come back to the ghetto. He was living a few weeks in this place. Because he hadn't got a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the paper we needed to-- to be in the ghetto. So he went to labor camp-- a camp. And we-- after the-- [? housing ?] [INAUDIBLE] in 1942, we get another couple with a child. Now, we-- how-- how we manage, I couldn't tell you. Because there was a small-- small room. We have two beds. They have two beds as well, and a tiny table. I couldn't tell you where the table was. And there was a-- to have a little bit of privacy, that we put a curtain in there. So I know there-- he has been partner with my brother in the business. His-- his-- his brother. I don't know what's happened to them both.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what's the name of the couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The company?

speaker: interviewer
text: The couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: The couple? Brown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brown.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been working for a while as-- as a manager to other famous-- I [? can't ?] [? remember ?] [? what. ?] Anyhow, my brother was a not-working partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto sealed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me what type of barrier--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --there was?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? The gates and the guard, what type of--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were the two gates. One gate was in [NON-ENGLISH]. And the other gate was in-- Hmm, in [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] on the-- on the-- the-- on the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], which I couldn't remember it. Near [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH] Going to the square of [? what ?] [? was ?] [? this ?] creek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a guard at the gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- from where was the guard? What nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have to leave the ghetto at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, I was going-- I have the-- the paper from-- [INAUDIBLE], I don't know what it's called-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --employment office. Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am allowed to go to the work at seven o'clock in the morning. And I have to come back whenever I were ready. But before nine o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what type of job were you doing? What work were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same job. I was-- I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --officially, I was a worker for-- for car repair, in workshop. But I-- I know these people from-- from the-- before the war. And we make an-- an arrangement that he will take all of my wages. And so it was not a big deal. But you know, when the Germans, they have five people. Give me give Jewish people. They couldn't care less. They said that's what they get, the same a [? privatization ?] with the Polish people. So he took-- took the-- the-- spirits, what I get twice a year, and working, clothing, and so on. He-- [? anyway ?] I-- I was from very short distance from him. When he expected somebody is coming, he give me a ring. And I jump on my bike and wait in place, waiting for the-- this coming back into my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you actually working?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- in a soda water factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: To make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Oskar Schindler, I meet-- I met him-- met him later. But before I met him, after the [INAUDIBLE] in '42, I find out that I was without place to work. Because I was one worker. And there was a new law that there have to be at least five. I have to have one-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --Ukrainian soldier who was escorting us and stay over there. And I was in a very bad position because after the [NON-ENGLISH], I haven't got-- I haven't got a place to work. It was very danger. I fortunately have a friend of mine who was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --a kind of [? porter ?] in-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --employment office. He told me it is very bad because there are only three places calling for worker. The one was the [NON-ENGLISH] and the other one was [NON-ENGLISH]. They were all-- all about 100 kilometers from Cracow. Doesn't-- doesn't-- I doesn't want to-- to part with my wife. And he told me there are other places I might [? find ?] [? work ?]. But people are not working. Not because they don't want to work, but because they are running away. They don't last a while anyway. And I tell him, listen, when-- I know the-- the factory from [INAUDIBLE], when I can find the people I had been dealing with, I can manage, maybe. Send me over there. I get-- they get a chance. And by this, then, I-- I come over. When I come over to the-- to the gate-- to the gate with-- the people who had been going-- going with me, on the-- on the gate, I find [INAUDIBLE], the engineer of press room. And he-- I know him from the war, very well. He was a alcoholic. But had a way very, very, very gentle and honest man. He was alcoholic. So it doesn't matter. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I-- I'll have to stop you. Because we'll run out of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'll change tape and then you come back and tell me how you met Schindler.
